03 Hooks Deep Dive — Not Magic, Just Linked Lists

← Back to lessons

You use hooks every day. Now see how they work internally. Once you understand that hooks are stored as a linked list on the fiber node, the "rules of hooks" stop being arbitrary restrictions and become obvious consequences of the implementation.

The Internal Model: Hooks Are Array Slots

When React calls your component function, it has a pointer to the component's fiber node. That fiber has a memoizedState property that points to a linked list of hook states. Each useState, useEffect, useMemo call maps to one node in that list.

// Your component:
function ArticleEditor() {
  const [title, setTitle] = useState('');        // Hook slot 0
  const [body, setBody] = useState('');          // Hook slot 1
  const [isDirty, setIsDirty] = useState(false); // Hook slot 2
  useEffect(() => { /* autosave */ }, [body]);   // Hook slot 3

  return /* ... */;
}

// What the fiber stores internally:
fiber.memoizedState = {
  memoizedState: '',           // title value
  queue: { pending: null },    // state update queue
  next: {
    memoizedState: '',         // body value
    queue: { pending: null },
    next: {
      memoizedState: false,    // isDirty value
      queue: { pending: null },
      next: {
        memoizedState: {       // useEffect state
          create: () => { /* autosave */ },
          deps: [''],
          destroy: undefined,
        },
        next: null
      }
    }
  }
}

Now the "rules of hooks" make perfect sense. React reads hooks by walking this linked list in order. On the first render, it creates the list. On every subsequent render, it reads from the list by position. If you put a hook inside a condition, the list length or order changes between renders, and React reads the wrong state for the wrong hook. It's not a stylistic preference — it's a data structure constraint.

Rules of Hooks, Explained by Implementation

Rule: Only call hooks at the top level

Broken: Conditional hook
function Profile({ user }) {
  const [name, setName] = useState('');

  // BROKEN: hook count changes
  if (user.isAdmin) {
    const [role, setRole] = useState('admin');
  }

  // On render where isAdmin=false,
  // this reads slot 1 — but slot 1
  // is supposed to be "role", which
  // doesn't exist. Hooks are shifted.
  useEffect(() => { /* ... */ }, []);
}
Correct: Always call, conditionally use
function Profile({ user }) {
  const [name, setName] = useState('');

  // Always called — same slot every render
  const [role, setRole] = useState('admin');

  // Conditionally USE the value, not the hook
  const displayRole = user.isAdmin ? role : null;

  // Hook order is always: 0, 1, 2
  useEffect(() => { /* ... */ }, []);
}

Rule: Only call hooks from React functions

Hooks rely on React's internal "current fiber" pointer. When React calls your component, it sets this pointer. Regular JavaScript functions don't have a fiber, so calling useState from a utility function would crash — there's no fiber to attach the hook to. Custom hooks work because they're called from a component function, inheriting its fiber context.

useState Internals

Here's a simplified version of how useState actually works:

// Simplified useState implementation
let currentFiber = null;
let hookIndex = 0;

function useState(initialValue) {
  const fiber = currentFiber;
  const oldHook = fiber.alternate?.memoizedState?.[hookIndex];

  const hook = {
    state: oldHook ? oldHook.state : initialValue,
    queue: [],
  };

  // Process queued updates from previous renders
  const actions = oldHook ? oldHook.queue : [];
  actions.forEach(action => {
    hook.state = typeof action === 'function'
      ? action(hook.state)  // functional update
      : action;             // direct value
  });

  const setState = (action) => {
    hook.queue.push(action);
    // Schedule a re-render of this component
    scheduleRerender(fiber);
  };

  fiber.hooks[hookIndex] = hook;
  hookIndex++;

  return [hook.state, setState];
}

Why functional updates exist

// Direct update — uses the value from THIS render's closure
setCount(count + 1);
setCount(count + 1);
setCount(count + 1);
// Result: count + 1 (not count + 3!)
// Because all three read the same `count` from this render

// Functional update — uses the latest state
setCount(prev => prev + 1);
setCount(prev => prev + 1);
setCount(prev => prev + 1);
// Result: count + 3 (each reads from the queue)

Batching: React batches multiple setState calls into a single re-render. In React 18+, all setState calls are batched automatically, even inside setTimeout, promises, and event handlers. This means React doesn't re-render between those three setCount calls — it processes all the queued actions, then re-renders once with the final state.

useEffect Internals

useEffect is not "componentDidMount for function components." It's a synchronization mechanism. The mental model: useEffect synchronizes your component with an external system.

// useEffect has three parts:
useEffect(
  () => {
    // 1. SETUP: runs after DOM paint
    const ws = new WebSocket(url);
    ws.onmessage = handleMessage;

    return () => {
      // 2. CLEANUP: runs before next effect or unmount
      ws.close();
    };
  },
  [url]  // 3. DEPENDENCIES: only re-run when these change
);

The dependency comparison

React compares dependencies using Object.is() (basically === for most cases). On each render, React compares each dependency value against the previous render's value. If any are different, the effect re-runs.

// This runs on EVERY render — objects are new references each time
useEffect(() => {
  fetchArticles(filters);
}, [{ category: 'politics', year: 2026 }]); // New object every render!

// Fix: use primitive values, or useMemo the object
useEffect(() => {
  fetchArticles(category, year);
}, [category, year]); // Strings/numbers compare by value

The empty dependency array [] doesn't mean "run once." It means "this effect has no dependencies, so it never needs to re-synchronize." In development with Strict Mode, React intentionally runs effects twice to help you find bugs in your cleanup logic. If your effect breaks when run twice, your cleanup function has a bug.

The timeline of effects

// Timeline of a component's life with effects:

// 1. First render
Component called → JSX returned → DOM updated → Paint → Setup runs

// 2. State change (re-render)
Component called → JSX returned → DOM updated → Paint →
  Cleanup (prev effect) → Setup (new effect)

// 3. Unmount
Cleanup (final) → Component removed from DOM

useRef: The Escape Hatch

useRef is the simplest hook internally. It creates an object with a .current property that persists across renders but doesn't trigger re-renders when changed.

// useRef is literally just this:
function useRef(initialValue) {
  const [ref] = useState({ current: initialValue });
  return ref;
}
// (The actual implementation is even simpler — no useState, just a hook slot)

Two main use cases:

// 1. DOM element access
function SearchInput() {
  const inputRef = useRef<HTMLInputElement>(null);

  useEffect(() => {
    inputRef.current?.focus(); // Access the actual DOM node
  }, []);

  return <input ref={inputRef} />;
}

// 2. Mutable value that survives re-renders (no re-render on change)
function StopWatch() {
  const intervalRef = useRef<number>(0);
  const [seconds, setSeconds] = useState(0);

  function start() {
    intervalRef.current = window.setInterval(() => {
      setSeconds(s => s + 1);
    }, 1000);
  }

  function stop() {
    clearInterval(intervalRef.current); // Access persists across renders
  }

  return /* ... */;
}

Custom Hooks: Composition That Scales

Custom hooks are just functions that call other hooks. They don't have special powers — they're a code organization pattern. But they're the most powerful abstraction in React because they let you extract and reuse stateful logic.

// Extract reusable logic into a custom hook
function useDebounce<T>(value: T, delay: number): T {
  const [debouncedValue, setDebouncedValue] = useState(value);

  useEffect(() => {
    const timer = setTimeout(() => setDebouncedValue(value), delay);
    return () => clearTimeout(timer); // Cleanup on change
  }, [value, delay]);

  return debouncedValue;
}

// Use it anywhere:
function ArticleSearch() {
  const [query, setQuery] = useState('');
  const debouncedQuery = useDebounce(query, 300);

  useEffect(() => {
    if (debouncedQuery) searchArticles(debouncedQuery);
  }, [debouncedQuery]);

  return <input value={query} onChange={e => setQuery(e.target.value)} />;
}

Real-world custom hook patterns

// useLocalStorage — persist state to localStorage
function useLocalStorage<T>(key: string, initialValue: T) {
  const [value, setValue] = useState<T>(() => {
    try {
      const stored = localStorage.getItem(key);
      return stored ? JSON.parse(stored) : initialValue;
    } catch {
      return initialValue;
    }
  });

  useEffect(() => {
    localStorage.setItem(key, JSON.stringify(value));
  }, [key, value]);

  return [value, setValue] as const;
}

// useMediaQuery — reactive media queries
function useMediaQuery(query: string): boolean {
  const [matches, setMatches] = useState(false);

  useEffect(() => {
    const mql = window.matchMedia(query);
    setMatches(mql.matches);

    const handler = (e: MediaQueryListEvent) => setMatches(e.matches);
    mql.addEventListener('change', handler);
    return () => mql.removeEventListener('change', handler);
  }, [query]);

  return matches;
}

// useFetch — data fetching with loading/error states
function useFetch<T>(url: string) {
  const [data, setData] = useState<T | null>(null);
  const [error, setError] = useState<Error | null>(null);
  const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true);

  useEffect(() => {
    const controller = new AbortController();
    setLoading(true);

    fetch(url, { signal: controller.signal })
      .then(res => res.json())
      .then(setData)
      .catch(err => {
        if (err.name !== 'AbortError') setError(err);
      })
      .finally(() => setLoading(false));

    return () => controller.abort(); // Cancel on unmount/url change
  }, [url]);

  return { data, error, loading };
}

Custom hooks are React's answer to mixins, HOCs, and render props. They solve the same problem — sharing stateful logic between components — but without the wrapper nesting, prop collision, or ceremony. A custom hook is just a function. It returns whatever you want. The only convention is the use prefix, which tells React's linter to check hook rules inside it.

Check Your Understanding

1. Where are hook states physically stored?

On the fiber node, in a linked list starting at fiber.memoizedState. Each hook call corresponds to one node in this list, ordered by call position.

2. Why does setCount(count + 1) called three times only increment by 1?

Because count is a closure over the current render's value. All three calls read the same count value and enqueue the same result. Use setCount(prev => prev + 1) for the functional form that chains properly.

3. Your useEffect runs on every render despite having a dependency array. What's the likely cause?

One of the dependencies is a new reference every render — typically an object or array literal, or a function defined inside the component. Objects are compared by reference (===), not by value. Extract the dependency to a useMemo, or use primitive values.

4. What's the difference between useRef and useState for storing a value?

Changing a ref's .current does NOT trigger a re-render. Changing state does. Use useRef for values you need to persist between renders but don't want to display (timers, previous values, DOM nodes). Use useState for values that affect the rendered output.

5. Why does React Strict Mode run effects twice in development?

To verify your cleanup function works correctly. If your effect subscribes to something, the cleanup should unsubscribe. Running setup/cleanup/setup exposes bugs where cleanup doesn't properly undo the setup. It's a correctness check, not a performance feature.

Exercise: Build These Custom Hooks

  1. useOnClickOutside(ref, callback): Detects clicks outside a referenced element. Handle cleanup properly.
  2. usePrevious(value): Returns the previous render's value of any variable. Hint: useRef + useEffect.
  3. useToggle(initial): Returns [value, toggle, setTrue, setFalse]. Simple, but practice the pattern.
  4. useAsync(asyncFn, deps): Wraps an async function with loading/error/data state. Handle race conditions with AbortController.
← Lesson 02: Fiber Lesson 04: State Management →
